title: PyTorch Eng Director: Promo Hacking, Industry Shifts, Regrets | John Myles White
guest_role: Ex-Director of Engineering at Meta Superintelligence Labs (PyTorch), Julia core contributor
key_takeaways: White is bullish on Meta as a stockholder but bearish on being an employee: the engineer labor market flipped from undersupply to perceived oversupply (except frontier-lab AI researchers), so all of Silicon Valley makes fewer retention sacrifices in comp, perks, and employee voice; layoff fear now dominates employee stress and makes perk cuts tolerable.

In Meta's AI Infra, literally everyone's first goal was promotion (unlike early Facebook, where it wasn't a thing); Monetization hired people with detailed promo-plan documents. Promo culture made teams ship projects everyone believed were bad and build maximally coupled systems to hit scope bars, decoupling promotion from skills that actually compound.

You can't unilaterally disarm in a promo-driven org ('it's totally irrational not to play the game'), but you can choose teams: PyTorch selected for love of the craft and held such a high leveling bar that its IC8s beat other teams' IC10s, which ironically made its promo cases more credible externally. White took ~20% less compensation to work somewhere he was proud of.

Career-growth story: joined Core Data Science as IC4 just as the 'emotional contagion' PNAS paper became Meta's worst PR disaster of the year (company-wide apology Q&A, regular meetings with the head of legal); later, all senior engineers on the experimentation team left within six months and he jumped from random IC5 to de facto TL of Deltoid 3, Meta's A/B testing framework — sudden-vacuum opportunities are underrated.

Statsig is essentially Meta's Deltoid, and Airflow literally is Meta's DataSwarm (its author quit and open-sourced it a week later) — internal big-tech infra is a repeatable startup playbook; White regrets not building a Deltoid startup years earlier.

Julia was built by MIT math people to destroy MATLAB; the case for it: naive R translations of plain C loops run 1,000-10,000x slower because dynamic languages pay for what users MIGHT do — R lets users redefine the brace operator, its lazy promise arguments are unneeded ~70-90% of the time (Vitek et al.), Python's inspect module can rewrite the symbol table. Fast languages have invariants; slow languages have runtime checks.

Academics who treat industry as a safe fallback interview visibly unwilling and assume competing with 'the dumb people' is trivial; he interviewed a CS professor who passed values between functions by reading REPL output and retyping it into other code.

Statistics book recommendations: anything by Larry Wasserman (hyper-intense honesty about assumptions and claims) and Peter Aronow's agnostic statistics book; statistics is 'the craziest field' — pure math sold as practical.

Statistical illiteracy plus goal culture is dangerous: a partner org blocked the Deltoid 3 launch because it flipped their barely-significant holdout win to non-significant, and a team he managed planned to ship a goal-hitting change on July 1 and delete it July 2 because 'we can't miss our goals' — he used his manager authority over ratings to stop it.

Biggest regret: never attending skip-level office hours held by Javi (Javier Olivan, now Meta COO) despite weekly prompting — fear-driven avoidance that hurt both sides, since directors+ are starved for ground truth. If a leader asks you to show up and talk, go.

Advice to younger self: be more ambitious — people over-rate others and under-rate themselves; successful people 'are not actually doing that much better than you. They just tried and you didn't try.'

notable_quotes.speaker: John Myles White
notable_quotes.quote: I am very bullish on Meta as a company that I am now a stockholder of, but not an employee.

notable_quotes.speaker: John Myles White
notable_quotes.quote: Everyone agreed the thing we were working on was bad. No one thought it would succeed, but people were like, oh, but I have to ship it because that's my promo bar.

notable_quotes.speaker: John Myles White
notable_quotes.quote: Fundamentally, any code that's slow is slow because it's doing stuff it doesn't need to do.

notable_quotes.speaker: John Myles White
notable_quotes.quote: They're not actually doing that much better than you. They just tried and you didn't try.
